---
title: "Principles of functional analysis"
output:
  html_document:
    css: style.css
    theme: cosmo
    toc: yes
    toc_float: yes
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

\newcommand{\W}{\mathcal{W}}
\newcommand{\R}{\mathbb{R}}
\newcommand{\C}{\mathbb{C}}
\newcommand{\Z}{\mathbb{Z}}
\newcommand{\para}[1]{\left( #1 \right)}
\newcommand{\sqbr}[1]{\left[ #1 \right]}
\newcommand{\E}{\mathbb{E}}
\newcommand{\Cov}{\mathrm{Cov}}
\newcommand{\Var}{\mathrm{Var}}

### Chapter 2

  1. Claim: The norm of a bounded linear functional $F$, $\para{\|F\|\equiv\sup_{x\in\underline{X}}\frac{|F(x)|}{\|x\|}}$ is equal to the smallest number $M$ satisfying
  
  $$|F(x)|\leq M\|x\| \ \ \forall \ x\in X.$$
  
  Proof: By definition there exists $M\geq0$ s.t. $|F(x)|\leq M\|x\| \ \ \forall \ x\in X.$ Set $M=\|F\|$ and assume $M'\leq M$ also satisfies the previous inequality. Then
  
  $$\frac{|F(x)|}{\|x\|}\leq M'\leq M=\sup_{x\in\underline{X}}\frac{|F(x)|}{\|x\|}.$$
  
  Hence, there is a sequence $\{x_n\}$ such that $\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}\frac{F(x_n)}{\|x_n\|}=M$ and $\frac{|F(x_n)|}{\|x_n\|}\leq M'$. Then, by continuity, $M=M'$. $\square$
  
  2.
